text: The patient in this video was diagnosed with a locally invasive rectal carcinoma and had received neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy prior to surgery. The defect seen on the right results from the abdominoperineal excision performed in the extra levator plane. the abdominal part of the procedure being performed laparoscopically.

The surgeon reconstructs the pelvic floor by reflecting the gracilis muscles from their distal insertion into the pelvic defect. The gracilis muscle, visible as the most superficial muscle in the adductor compartment of the thigh, is carefully positioned to restore pelvic integrity.

The surgeon marks the position of the gracilis muscle with a line from the ischial tuberosity to the medial condyle of the tibia. An incision is made eight centimeters below the ischial tuberosity, extending through subcutaneous tissue and deep fascia. The gracilis muscle is mobilized from surrounding areolar tissue using diathermy to cauterize small vessels, avoiding the long saphenous vein and medial cutaneous nerve. The distal tendon is isolated and divided with diathermy, allowing the muscle to be reflected. Dissection continues proximally along the muscle body, ligating small perforating vessels. The neurovascular bundle is visible entering the muscle, with the obturator nerve seen entering two centimeters proximal to this point.

The surgeon repeats the procedure on the contralateral side, highlighting the vascular pedicle. A tunnel is created between the peroneal defect and the medial compartment of the thigh using diathermy through the deep fascia. Forceps are used to pass the gracilis muscle through the tunnel into the peroneal defect, ensuring the muscle is not twisted to maintain its blood supply.

The surgeon closes the skin incision on the thigh in layers, placing a subcutaneous suction drain. The gracilis muscles are woven together and fixed to the distal sacrum and coccyx using permanent sutures. The tendons lie centrally with muscle bellies laterally, attaching to the origin of the levator ani, maintaining innervation to prevent atrophy.
Patients immobilized on day one avoid sitting directly on the wound through the use of specialist cushions. The drains are removed once mobility is independent.
